# SEV-1 INCIDENT - RELEASE BLOCKER
**Title:** Statements are filing some payments under the wrong kind of transaction, or dropping the payee name
**Owner:** Payments Operations
**Disposition:** Do not ship this release until it is resolved

## What customers reported

Over the past few days support has collected a run of tickets that all share one
shape: the money itself moved fine, but the app files the payment under a category
that does not match what the customer actually did, or shows the wrong name - or
no name at all - beside it. In every case the row that misbehaves carries one very
particular, uncommon form of raw statement text; everyday rows are labeled exactly
as before. The tickets below are anonymized, but the raw narration each customer
pasted is reproduced verbatim, because that raw text is what makes each case
reproducible.

> "I wrote a cheque against my account and the app just lists it as a general
> transfer now - nothing marks it as a cheque anymore. My earlier cheques were
> tagged correctly. The only thing sitting in that row's note is the cheque's own
> number, nothing else around it."

> "My salary came in and the amount is right, but where my employer's name should
> be the app now just prints the word `NACH`. The raw text on that row looks like
> `ACH-BD-NACH-ACME INDUSTRIES` - it used to lift the employer's name off the end
> of that reference, and now it is picking up the wrong piece of it."

> "On some transfers that come in, the sender's name is blank now. Those rows all
> share the same reference layout - a short block of letters, then a long string
> of digits, then the name - for example `GBHDL0123456789 RAVI KUMAR`. The name
> sitting at the end just is not being pulled through anymore; the name column
> comes out empty."

The same batch of complaints also covers two quieter cases that are about the
category rather than the name. The token confirmation charge a bank posts when a
new auto-debit mandate is set up - a small nominal amount, with the note flagging
it as a mandate - is being filed as an ordinary transfer instead of being
recognized as a verification entry. And a refund that came back on a card is
landing as a plain refund for some customers when it should read as a card
reversal; a customer on a different type of account sees the same kind of refund
labeled correctly, so the wrong label appears to depend on the account category.

## Scope of impact

Everyday activity - normal purchases, routine transfers, regular deposits - is
categorized and named correctly, which is precisely why this slipped through QA and
why our monitoring dashboards looked healthy. The breakage is confined to a small
set of unusual transaction forms - among them a note or reference that is exactly
a certain number of digits wide - each one hinging on some exact shape of the raw
row rather than anything about the customer, and each form individually uncommon. But they occur right across the customer base, so they
generate a steady trickle of "why is this labeled wrong" tickets and, more
damaging, they poison the spending-category summaries and payee histories that
several downstream features rely on. That combination is why we are treating it
as ship-blocking rather than a backlog item.

## What we need from engineering

The labeling path is fully deterministic and runs offline - hand it the same
transaction and it reproduces the same wrong answer every time - so each of these
is reproducible on demand and can be pinned down exactly. Get the affected forms
labeling and naming correctly again, and do it without disturbing any transaction
that is already labeled right today. A fix that rescues these cases but nudges the
behavior of ordinary, currently-correct transactions is not acceptable: the pass
condition is simply that the specific problem payments described above come out
right and everything else stays exactly where it is.

<thinking>
First, I need to locate the transaction processing logic where payment categories and payee names are determined. The error reports mention specific parsing issues with statement text, so I'll look for files related to categorization and parsing. Checking the codebase requires finding implementation files first. 

I'll use glob to search for Python files under src/transactions (common location for payment logic) and config/categories (potential categorization rules). This will help identify the exact files handling these classifications. The problems described relate to both category determination and payee extraction logic, so I must find both components.

Next, I'll need to inspect how these files parse regular expressions or string patterns from transaction references. The reports state issues come from exact text formats not matching current parsing rules. For example, 'NACH' in references breaking name extraction and check numbers being misinterpreted. I'll verify the existing regex patterns for edge cases that aren't handled properly.

Since these are labeling errors affecting historical data categorization, there's likely either:
1. Incorrect order of parsing rules (more specific cases before general ones)
2. Missing regex patterns for these exact formats
3. Regex patterns that unintentionally match both correct and broken cases

I'll need to validate current matching logic against test cases from the report - like 'GBHDL\d{14} [NAME]' references not capturing names and ACH transactions mislabeled as "general transfer". The goal is to fix the faulty patterns without disrupting existing correct entries.

Finally, after identifying which patterns need adjustment, I'll plan to:
1. Add regex entries that match the failing formats early in parse order
2. Adjust matching groups for name/category extraction
3. Verify fixes don't create regressions through unit tests
The first step is mapping the code structure to locate where these classifications happen.
</thinking>

<thinking>
The initial glob search in src/transactions and config/categories showed no files, but the categorization logic must exist somewhere in the codebase. I need to verify alternative locations since transaction processing could be in:

1. Payment processing modules (maybe under src/payments)
2. Transaction parsers (src/parsers or src/processing)
3. Shared utilities (src/utils or src/common)

I'll check for payment_processor.py and transaction_parser.py which are common filenames for this functionality. Since the error connects payment categorization to statement text parsing, configuration files might exist in config/types or config/rules directories. Need to confirm these exist before exploring further.

The previous search for.yaml failed, perhaps configuration uses JSON or YML without.yaml extension. Checking both common config locations with multiple extensions will help locate categorization rules that determine transaction labels. This is critical since the problem stems from regex matches in raw text, likely defined in config files rather than inline code logic.
</thinking>

<thinking>
After multiple targeted glob attempts found no results, I need to broaden the search to capture both file location possibilities (app/transactions, app/categorization) and common configuration formats (yaml/yml, json). The problem requires locating where both transaction parsing logic and categorization rules are defined. 

The README mentions a "transaction parser infrastructure" - this suggests existence of parsing code that may dynamically load configurations. The error stems from misclassification rules failing to capture specific statement formats. Since prior searches in standard directories failed, checking the root directory for categorization tables (categories.yml or transaction_rules.json) would be prudent. These might be top-level config files referenced in multiple components.

The failure to find files in src/parsers means the parsing logic could be within statement_parser.py under a common directory. The check_number example shows regex-based handling in Python code, so locating where these patterns are defined is critical. Remaining combinations need verification to ensure all possibilities are exhausted before alternative tactics.
</thinking>

<thinking>
After multiple attempts to locate categorization and parsing logic through file structure searches failed, I need to pivot to content-based discovery. The problem involves regex patterns that incorrectly categorize transactions based on raw statement text. Using grep to search all Python files for regex patterns matching the failing cases ("cheque number", "ACH-BD-NACH", "GBHDL0123...NAME") can pinpoint the exact code handling these classifications.

The check-related categorization problem suggests there's regex looking for "cheque" keywords - checking for.match() or.search() calls with patterns like "cheque\s+number". Similarly, ACH/NACH references would use patterns containing ACH, and the GBHDL reference error implies a regex expecting a different name extraction format.

The critical files from the README examples (like payment_processor.py) must exist somewhere. By searching for these specific filenames across the entire codebase with grep, I can validate their actual paths. The configuration files mentioned might not use standard naming conventions (categories.yaml vs. categorization.cfg), so searching for "rules" or "keywords" in configuration files would reveal their actual location.

Next step: Use grep to find:
1. Payment categorization regex patterns ("cheque", "transfer_types", "payment_rules")
2. Payee extraction logic ("name_extract", "payee_regex")
3. Transaction processor imports to locate main business logic
This will bypass failing directory assumptions and locate the actual code handling parsing.
</thinking>
